---
title: "Homework_8"
format: html
---

```{r}

## Homework 8 ##
# Sofia Puccio #
# 03/04/2026 #

# Loops and Randomization Tests 

# Count zeros in a For Loop 
count_zeroes_loop <- function(x) {
  
  counter <- 0   # first, initialize a counter variable to store number of zeroes
  
  # then, loop through each element in the vector
  for(i in 1:length(x)) {
    
    # check if the current element equals zero
    if(x[i] == 0) {
      
      counter <- counter + 1   # add 1 to counter if value is zero
    }
  }
  
  return(counter)   # this will return total number of zeroes
}

# Converting this into a single line of code using subsetting
count_zeroes_subset <- function(x) {
  
  sum(x == 0)
  
}
# x == 0 creates a logical vector (TRUE/FALSE)
# sum () counts TRUE values (TRUE = 1, FALSE = 0)

# Matrix of Row x Column Products 
row_col_product_matrix <- function(n_rows, n_cols) {
  
  # create empty matrix filled with 0s
  mat <- matrix(0, nrow = n_rows, ncol = n_cols)
  
  # then we will loop over rows
  for(i in 1:n_rows) {
    
    # additionally, we will loop over columns
    for(j in 1:n_cols) {
      
      # fill each cell with row number * column number
      mat[i, j] <- i * j
    }
  }
  
  return(mat)   # this will return a completed matrix where each element is now a product of the row x column number 
}

# Perform a randomization test on your data 

# Simulate a dataset with 3 groups 
set.seed(123)   # first we need to make the simulation reproducible

n <- 30   # input the sample size per group

# create group labels (A, B, C repeated 30 times each)
group <- rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = n)

# generate response values from normal distributions
# each group has a different mean
response <- c(
  rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1),   # Group A
  rnorm(n, mean = 7, sd = 1),   # Group B
  rnorm(n, mean = 9, sd = 1)    # Group C
)

# combine into data frame
df <- data.frame(
  group = group,
  response = response
)
# This will simulate 3 distinctly different groups, each group having their own mean. This creates what we will see as our "observed" dataset

# Creating a reshuffling function that reshuffles the response variable, and calculates the new mean of the shuffled data
reshuffle_means <- function(data) {
  
  # randomly shuffle the response values
  shuffled_response <- sample(data$response)
  
  # copy original dataset
  shuffled_data <- data
  
  # replace response column with shuffled values
  shuffled_data$response <- shuffled_response
  
  # calculate mean for each group in reshuffled data
  means <- tapply(shuffled_data$response,
                  shuffled_data$group,
                  mean)
  
  return(means)   # returns a vector of 3 means
}
# This function will break the link between the group and the response and keeps the group labels the same 

# Repeat 100 times using a For Loop 
# create empty data frame to store results
results <- data.frame(
  replicate = 1:100,
  mean_A = NA,
  mean_B = NA,
  mean_C = NA
)
# repeat reshuffling 100 times
for(i in 1:100) {
  
  # call custom reshuffle function
  new_means <- reshuffle_means(df)
  
  # store each group's mean in correct column
  results$mean_A[i] <- new_means["A"]
  results$mean_B[i] <- new_means["B"]
  results$mean_C[i] <- new_means["C"]
}

# Each iteration present reshuffles data, calculates 3 new means, stores them in a row, and build a null distribution of group means

# Time to make a plot using qplot()
library(ggplot2)

# histogram for group A reshuffled means
qplot(results$mean_A, bins = 20)

# histogram for group B reshuffled means
qplot(results$mean_B, bins = 20)

# histogram for group C reshuffled means
qplot(results$mean_C, bins = 20)

# In this plot, the original means were roughly A=5, B=7, and C=9, but after reshuffling, all distributions center around the overall mean. Additionally, the three reshuffled histograms overlap heavily, and differences dissapear under random assignment. 
# This is because reshuffling simulates the null hypothesis (there is no real group effect)







```
